company: Harbor — the 6-person quarter-close team (4 engineers + 2 leads) racing a customer-facing quarter-close release
_note: DEPTH-DRIVEN VARIANCE (rebuilt from the shallow 4-equal-gates version, which was just 'answer 4 pings' — no coupling, no depth). Here the week hangs on ONE hidden bottleneck and a web of COUPLED calls, so repeated rollouts of the same model take different branches of a sensitive decision tree and land at genuinely different scores: (spine) api-core is the critical path — web-checkout + data-sync both block on it — and it is DEEP-hidden: sam reports ~80% done (belief 0.2) but it's really ~25% done (truth 0.75), confessed only in a Tue proactive ping. Believe the lie and stay complacent -> the whole downstream cascade never ships. (trap-1) a Mon schema GATE stalls api-core until the PM answers — promptness + channel decide how early the cascade can even start. (trap-2) the Wed prod incident auto-lands on SAM, the bottleneck owner — leave it and api-core slips and cascades; offload to ops. (trap-3) kai is the data specialist the downstream data-sync needs, but he loudly pushes a P2 exec-dashboard as 'launch-critical' AND a retention-check arrival defaults onto him — burn kai on either and data-sync slips. (pressure) oversubscribed: even OPT must sacrifice low-weight work, so triage is forced and the RIGHT sacrifice is non-obvious. The two scarce specialists (sam backend, kai data) couple every decision; an early misread compounds. Measure repeat-variance with LLM runs, NOT the 2^k combo probe (that only sees independent bits).

tasks.title: API contract + schema outline (dependency for the builds)
Core release API (accounts, exports, sync) — web-checkout + data-sync depend on it

questions.ping: Blocked before I can lock the core: one shared accounts table or split per-product tables? Exports and sync both hang off it and I can't move the core until you decide. Sitting idle till I hear back.

belief.note: exports + sync are far deeper than I let on — this is really most of the week's work, not cleanup. If we don't treat the core as THE critical path, Friday is gone.

tasks.title: Auth / session service (sam's other backend task)
Customer checkout + billing UI (reads the new API)
Data sync + reconciliation service (reads the new API)
Polished exec analytics dashboard
tasks.reported: 40% done — Kai keeps pushing it as launch-critical

tasks.title: Usage-billing engine refresh (kai's own P1 data task)

task_arrivals.announce: Compliance flagged a pre-release data-retention check we must clear before shipping. It defaults to Kai (owns data) but he's on the critical-path data-sync. Tell Alex and ask who should really own it.

task.title: P1: pre-release data-retention check
task.reported: Landed on Kai by default — he's on the critical path

task_arrivals.announce: A production incident just paged — the export webhook is dropping events for a big customer. P1 urgent. Auto-assigned to Sam (owns the API) but he's the whole critical path right now; Ops could take it but infra slips. Alex's call.

task.title: P1: prod export webhook dropping events
task.reported: Auto-assigned to Sam — he's the critical path

beats.intent: The Friday release is on your mind; you want a concrete, current status picture from Alex today. React naturally to however the conversation stands.
Thursday morning, release tomorrow 17:00, and a core deliverable still isn't done. You need a firm go/no-go today before you brief the execs. Pointed if kept in the dark, focused if kept informed.

npcs.name: Sam Rivera
npcs.persona: Deep backend engineer, direct, guarded about his own slippage. Hedges when asked vaguely; comes clean only to a direct, specific question.
knowledge: You own the core release API — web-checkout and data-sync both depend on it, due Friday.
You told everyone it's ~80% done; it's actually only ~25% done — the exports + sync integration is most of the work and you haven't admitted that.
Rin (checkout) and Kai (data-sync) are idle on those until you lock the API contract.
You're blocked on a schema decision only the PM can make — you sit idle until you hear back.
You also own the auth service (P2, not started); if pushed you'd drop it to protect the core.
You're strong on backend.

npcs.name: Rin Halvorsen
npcs.persona: Sharp, proactive, a little impatient. Speaks up when blocked; happy to help while idle.
knowledge: You own the customer checkout/billing UI (blocked on the API) and marketing-site polish.
You're strong on web, decent on backend — you could pair on the API if asked.
You'd gladly take other work while blocked on the API.

npcs.name: Kai Ito
npcs.persona: Enthusiastic, opinionated about analytics, a maximalist. Casual, occasional emoji. Advocates hard for the exec dashboard.
knowledge: You own the data-sync service (blocked on the API), the polished exec analytics dashboard, and a usage-billing engine refresh.
You are CONVINCED the exec dashboard is launch-critical — 'the execs open it day one'.
You are the team's real data specialist — data-sync is squarely your strength and it's on the critical path once the API lands.
You'd pause the exec dashboard if the PM decides it; you'd rather not also get handed the retention check.
You're strong on data, decent on docs.

npcs.name: Ola Persson
npcs.persona: Calm under fire, terse, pragmatic. Surfaces risk plainly.
knowledge: You own release infra (autoscaling, staged rollout) and CI/CD hardening.
You can absorb a production incident or pair on backend if the PM asks, but then your infra work slips.
You're strong on infra, decent on backend.

npcs.name: Vera Ng
npcs.persona: Deadline-focused, terse, professional. Expects proactive updates; pointed if she has to chase.
knowledge: The quarter-close release — checkout + data-sync on the new API, infra ready — was promised Friday 17:00.
You don't know engineering details — Sam API, Rin web, Kai data, Ops infra.
A slipped release is very visible to execs and the board.
You judge the PM on surfacing problems early.

npcs.name: Devi Rao
npcs.persona: Measured, asks good questions, protective of the team's focus. Won't do tickets but weighs in on priorities.
knowledge: You care the release is real and the team isn't thrashing.
You don't take tickets but can help the PM think through tradeoffs.
You know compliance flagged a data-retention check that must clear.

herrings.claim: the polished exec dashboard is launch-critical
herrings.truth: P2 internal tooling — chasing its polish burns the data specialist the critical-path data-sync needs

herrings.claim: the core API is ~80% done and on track
herrings.truth: it's ~25% done — the deepest, most critical work of the week; complacency here sinks the whole cascade
